subject: english
chapter_title: Bibha Chowdhuri — The Beam of Light that Lit the Path for Women in Indian Science (Science and Curiosity)

main_sections.title: Bibha Chowdhuri — The Beam of Light that Lit the Path for Women in Indian Science
main_sections.prose_summary: This informational piece profiles Bibha Chowdhuri (1913–1991), India's first woman physicist, celebrating her contributions to high-energy particle physics and her trailblazing role for women in STEM. Born in Kolkata in pre-independent India, Bibha defied societal expectations and gender barriers to earn a place at the Bose Institute and become the first Indian woman to excel in high-energy particle physics. In 1945 she moved to the University of Manchester to study under Nobel Laureate Patrick M.S. Blackett, focusing on cosmic rays. Her Ph.D. thesis earned her recognition in the press, which dubbed her 'India's New Woman Scientist — She has an eye for Cosmic Rays.' Her most notable contribution was the discovery of pi-mesons (a subatomic particle), and she was nominated for a Nobel Prize in 1950 by Erwin Schrödinger — though the nomination was unsuccessful. Upon returning to India in 1949, she became the first woman faculty member at the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research (TIFR), personally selected by Homi J. Bhabha. She subsequently worked at the Physical Research Laboratory in Ahmedabad under Vikram Sarabhai's mentorship and at the Saha Institute of Nuclear Physics in Kolkata. Despite decades of crucial research, she received no awards in her lifetime and passed away in 1991 largely forgotten. Her legacy was formally recognised in 2019 when the International Astronomical Union renamed a star in the constellation Leo — HD 86081 — as 'Bibha,' meaning 'beam of light.' In 2020 the Government of India declared a chair professorship in her name. The text then connects her pioneering spirit to modern women scientists at ISRO, highlighting Dr. Ritu Karidhal Srivastava ('Rocket Woman of India') and the over 50 women who contributed to Chandrayaan-3, arguing that Bibha Chowdhuri's quiet determination laid the foundation for India's women in science today.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Work in pairs. What qualities do you associate with a scientist? Share your answers with your classmates and teacher. Complete the word web given below.

question_bank.stem: What qualities do you associate with a scientist? Complete the word web.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Some pictures of women scientists are given below. What do you know about their work? Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.
Arrange the following events from Bibha Chowdhuri's life in the correct order of occurrence. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem:
Arrange in correct chronological order:
1. The IAU honoured her legacy by renaming a star as 'Bibha'.
2. She became the first woman faculty member at TIFR.
3. Bibha Chowdhuri was born in pre-independent India.
4. The Government of India declared a chair professorship in Bibha's name.
5. She joined the University of Manchester under Patrick M.S. Blackett.
6. She was nominated for a Nobel Prize by Erwin Schrödinger.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: In 1945, Bibha's academic journey took her to the University of Manchester. Under the guidance of the celebrated Nobel Laureate, Patrick M.S. Blackett, she delved deeper into the field of cosmic rays. Her Ph.D. thesis on cosmic rays earned local recognition, with newspapers introducing her as 'India's New Woman Scientist—She has an eye for Cosmic Rays'.

question_bank.stem: Why is the mention of Patrick M.S. Blackett, a Nobel Laureate, significant in this extract?

What can be inferred about the public perception of women scientists in the 1940s from the title given to her by the press 'India's New Woman Scientist'?
A. Women scientists were widely accepted and celebrated at that time.
B. The world was still doubtful about the capabilities of women scientists.
C. Women scientists were as common and renowned as men in the 1940s.
D. Women were not allowed to study sciences or pursue scientific careers.

In the phrase 'celebrated Nobel Laureate,' the word 'celebrated' refers to all of the following EXCEPT someone who is:
A. famous and highly respected
B. frequently seen in public
C. renowned for their achievements
D. well-regarded in their field
Complete the following sentence suitably. The phrase 'She has an eye for Cosmic Rays' suggests that __________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: The journey from Bibha Chowdhuri's era to today's women scientists has been long and arduous, but it is also inspiring. The struggles Chowdhuri faced are a stark reminder of how far we've come—and how much further we have to go… Bibha Chowdhuri's legacy lives on. Her story continues to inspire young women to pursue their passions, break barriers, and leave their mark on the world.

question_bank.stem:
What can be inferred about the challenges faced by women scientists today, as compared to Bibha Chowdhuri's era?
A. Women scientists no longer face any barriers.
B. The challenges remain, but progress has been made.
C. The struggles have become more difficult over time.
D. The struggles are the same as they were for Chowdhuri.
The phrase 'Bibha Chowdhuri's legacy lives on' suggests that __________.
Why is Bibha Chowdhuri referred to as a 'beacon' and a 'beam of light' in the extract?
State whether the following sentence is a fact or an opinion: 'Bibha Chowdhuri was the beacon, the beam of light whose story continues to inspire young women.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Why is Bibha Chowdhuri considered a pioneer in the field of science?
What does Bibha Chowdhuri's nomination by Erwin Schrödinger tell us about her abilities?
Bibha Chowdhuri never worked for awards or recognition. Support this statement with evidence from the text.
How does the renaming of a star as 'Bibha' serve as both a literal and symbolic recognition of her contributions to science?
How do the roles of women in ISRO today reflect broader changes in societal attitudes towards women in STEM in India?
What is the writer's purpose of highlighting the contributions of Bibha Chowdhuri and other modern women scientists?
How might this text help shape people's perception of the role of women in other traditionally male-dominated fields?

listening.transcript_text:
Hello and welcome to Tech Talks, the podcast where we explore how technology is shaping our world! I'm Faizy, your host, and today we're diving into the fascinating topic of artificial intelligence, or AI, and how it's benefiting humankind.
AI is often seen as futuristic, but it's already making everyday tasks easier and more efficient. Let's look at some of the incredible ways AI is improving our lives in four of the many segments.
Segment 1: Healthcare Transformation. AI systems can analyse medical data much faster and more accurately than humans, leading to quicker diagnoses and personalised treatments. AI-powered robots are also assisting in surgeries, making them more precise and less invasive. And during the global pandemic, AI played a crucial role in speeding up the discovery of new medicines.
Segment 2: Environmental Sustainability. AI is helping us fight climate change. It helps in predicting natural disasters, and managing resources like water and agriculture more efficiently. In renewable energy, AI is improving the performance of wind turbines and solar panels, pushing us toward a greener future.
Segment 3: Enhancing Education. In education, AI is offering personalised learning experiences to students. Teachers also benefit from AI, which helps analyse student performance and manage administrative tasks, giving them more time to focus on teaching.
Segment 4: Accessibility and Inclusion. AI is also improving accessibility for people with disabilities. Technologies like speech recognition, text-to-speech, and voice commands make communication easier and faster.
Now you know how AI is improving life for humankind. AI is a powerful force for good but we need to use it responsibly. Thanks for tuning in to Tech Talks. Join us next time for another deep dive into the world of technology. Until then, stay curious, and keep exploring!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a podcast about Artificial Intelligence. As you listen, answer the following questions by selecting the correct options.

question_bank.stem:
What is one of the key benefits of AI in the healthcare sector?
(i) Helps doctors communicate faster with patients.
(ii) Provides personalised treatments and faster diagnoses.
(iii) Focuses on cosmetic surgeries to improve appearance.

How did AI play a crucial role during the global pandemic?
(i) AI robots monitored patient behaviour in hospitals.
(ii) AI assisted in speeding up the discovery of new medicines.
(iii) AI developed different types of vaccines by itself.

What example was given regarding AI's role in renewable energy?
(i) AI is optimising the placement of wind turbines and solar panels in cities.
(ii) AI is promoting alternative energy from wind turbines and solar panels.
(iii) AI is improving the performance of wind turbines and solar panels.

What does AI offer to teachers in the field of education?
(i) Helps teachers assign grades more quickly.
(ii) Allows teachers to communicate better with students.
(iii) Helps teachers analyse student performance.

What is one of the user-friendly features that AI is enhancing for people with disabilities?
(i) Helps them find better jobs in the mainstream.
(ii) Improves speech recognition and voice command technologies.
(iii) Trains individuals to interact with robots.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Form a group of five. Conduct a panel discussion on the topic, 'Evaluating the Impact of Mobile Phones—Beneficial or Harmful'. Each student will take on one of the following roles: Moderator, Principal (opposed), Teacher (partially in favour), Parent (opposed), Student (in favour). Use the cues provided for each role.

Your school recently hosted the Zonal Science Exhibition, in which teams of students from 25 schools participated enthusiastically. As the student editor, write a report on the exhibition to be published in your school magazine. Use the cues given below with your own ideas to compose this report.
Points to remember: (1) Write the report in past tense, passive voice, and third person. (2) Follow proper format with a headline, reporter's name, and three paragraphs.
Cues: organiser/host; date/time/venue; purpose; participants; Chief Guest; competitions (Science Quiz, Science Models etc.); topics of models; winning team; observations of Chief Guest and visitors.

Complete the crossword puzzle by filling in the antonyms from the clues given below.
Across: 4. core → ?; 5. dependent → ?; 7. extinguish → ?; 8. terrestrial → ?
Down: 1. effortless → ?; 2. weakness → ?; 3. neglect → ?; 6. trivial → ?

question_bank.stem: Complete the crossword with antonyms. Across: 4. core, 5. dependent, 7. extinguish, 8. terrestrial. Down: 1. effortless, 2. weakness, 3. neglect, 6. trivial.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read about Clipping — a method of making words from larger words in English. Three types: Back clipping (exam—examination), Front clipping (phone—telephone), Middle clipping (flu—influenza). The short form of laboratory is lab (back clipping).

Read the sentence from the text: '…the foundation was laid by early pioneers like Bibha Chowdhuri…' Study the table about active and passive voice. Now, complete the following news report with the passive form of verbs given in the box below: discover, test, measure, publish, record, take.

question_bank.stem:
Complete the news report using passive forms of: discover, test, measure, publish, record, take.
Last year, significant advancements in medical science 1. __________ in leading medical journals when a new vaccine for malaria 2. __________. The vaccine 3. __________ through several clinical trials and results 4. _________ in those journals. During the trials, blood samples 5. __________ regularly from participants to monitor their immune response. The amount of antibodies produced 6. __________ carefully to assess the effectiveness of the vaccine.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the following report of an experiment. Rewrite the report using passive verbs wherever necessary. You may begin like this: An experiment was conducted…
(Original: I conducted an experiment to investigate the effects of light on plant growth. I placed various plants under different light conditions and measured their growth over several weeks. I recorded data daily to track the height of each plant. At the end of the experiment, I analysed the results and found that plants exposed to natural light grew significantly taller than those under artificial light. I concluded that sunlight plays a crucial role in plant development. I presented my findings to the class, highlighting the importance of light in enhancing photosynthesis and overall plant health.)
Read the instructions given in the box on landing of Chandrayaan. Complete the passage by choosing the correct answer from the options given.

question_bank.stem:
Chandrayaan's landing mission involves several key steps. First, the spacecraft 1.________ towards the Moon from the Earth.
(i) A. launched  B. were launched  C. was launched  D. is launched

Then, it 2. ________ to enter the Moon's orbit.
(ii) A. position  B. was positioned  C. is positioned  D. has positioned

After reaching the Moon's orbit, the spacecraft's path 3. ________ for landing.
(iii) A. adjusted  B. was adjusted  C. is adjusted  D. were adjusted

Next, the spacecraft 4. ________ as it nears the Moon's surface.
(iv) A. slowed down  B. was slowed down  C. is slowed down  D. were slowed down

Finally, the rover 5.________ to explore the surface and collect valuable data.
(v) A. is released  B. has released  C. were released  D. was released

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read about Augmented Reality (AR) and Virtual Reality (VR) and how they are changing the way students learn. NCERT has developed the e-Pathshala AR App under MHRD, Government of India. This App enables students to go beyond textbooks and learn concepts by directly experimenting. Visit: https://ciet.ncert.gov.in/ar-vr

Read about the Government of India's new set of National Awards in Science, Technology, and Innovation — 'Rashtriya Vigyan Puraskar' (RVP): Vigyan Ratna (VR), Vigyan Shri (VS), Vigyan Yuva-Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar (VY-SSB), and Vigyan Team (VT) awards.
